For each case: materialize an isolated workspace (sandbox)
Optionally run pre-patch tests/static analysis only if --allow-local-execution and reveal flags are set (blind by default)
Build the blind reviewer context (case id, stack, diff, bounded files; no title/category/severity/ground-truth/test output)
Reviewer.review(context) returns findings and an optional patch
Score detection deterministically (score_case): concept/file/line/severity matching, false-positive penalty
Select the single case-level repair patch (_select_case_patch; ambiguous if multiple competing finding patches)
Apply the patch in a copied workspace with integrity guards (no tests/config/protected/absolute/.. paths)
Copy hidden tests in, snapshot them, execute required tests via Docker or trusted-local with read-only test mount and resource limits
Run structural validators (AST/static/source-text) on the patched workspace
Score deterministically and attribute evidence: detect tampering, derive case_status, repair_confidence, execution_backend, execution_validated
Aggregate metrics; classify run_status (complete/partial/invalid/failed/legacy) and execution_backend weakest-link first

code-review-arena.failureMode: Existing AI code-review benchmarks tend to credit a sharp-looking review comment as success without checking whether a proposed fix actually applies and passes tests, and reviewers can pass on leaked metadata (title/category/severity) or on pre-patch failing-test output that discloses expected values. They also rarely defend against adversarial reviewers or tampered benchmark packs.
code-review-arena.howItWorks: Each case is a seeded pull request (before/after code, a pr.diff, hidden tests, a reference.patch, and a case.yaml declaring ground truth, scoring weights, required tests, and structural validators). The harness sends the reviewer a blind payload (diff plus bounded files, no ground truth or descriptive metadata, no pre-patch test output), takes the returned findings and optional patch, scores detection at file granularity (with line localization reported separately) using deterministic lexical/AST heuristics, then applies the single repair patch in an isolated copied workspace, runs the required tests (Docker-isolated or trusted-local) with the hidden tests pinned read-only and tamper-detected, and runs structural validators. Detection and validation are scored separately so a correct-looking comment with a non-working fix scores high on detection but zero on validated_case_rate. Every run records a manifest (harness version, git SHA+dirty flag, pack checksum) and seals a content-addressed evidence bundle that arena verify-run can later check.

code-review-arena.oneLiner: A local, execution-backed benchmark harness that measures whether an AI code-review agent can both detect a seeded pull-request bug and supply a patch that actually applies and passes tests, scoring detection separately from validation.
code-review-arena.problem: AI code-review benchmarks often reward a sharp-looking comment without confirming that any proposed fix actually works, and reviewers can game them via leaked metadata or pre-patch test output. Code Review Arena instead applies each suggested patch in an isolated workspace, runs the required hidden tests and structural validators, and scores detection separately from validation so a plausible-looking review is never mistaken for a working repair. It runs entirely locally and is model-agnostic, treating both reviewers and benchmark packs as potentially adversarial.

code-review-arena.thesis: Trustworthy evaluation of AI code reviewers requires execution-backed proof of a working fix, not credit for a plausible comment — so detection and validation must be scored separately, the reviewer payload must be blind, and both reviewers and benchmark packs must be treated as adversarial with tamper-evident, reproducible runs.
limitations: Packs are curated and small: 30 cases total across v1, audit_v1, and audit_v2 (10 each).
Concept matching is lexical (curated keywords), not semantic, so well-paraphrased findings can be under-credited (execution metrics are unaffected).
Structural validators are comment-stripped, hand-authored heuristics that may reject alternate valid repairs and do not strip string literals; tests are the real gate.
Passing tests is execution evidence, not proof of complete correctness.
This is a local audit harness, not a large-scale public ranking; the API server is explicitly not hardened for public exposure.
No real external-model results ship in the repo — only deterministic controls and the reference-patch reviewer; running a real model requires the custom-command or HTTP reviewer.
Per-bug repair attribution is suite-level for multi-bug cases (every seeded bug shares the suite's repair verdict); per-bug oracle mapping is noted as a later refinement.
No image assets exist in the repository (no screenshots/diagrams/logos).

Debug-Brief.repo: /tmp/portfolio-audit/Debug-Brief (local clone; GitHub remote referenced as github.com/harihkk/Debug-Brief)
Debug-Brief.oneLiner: A local-first, AI-free CLI that records your debugging commands, outcomes, notes, and Git changes, then generates evidence-backed Markdown reports for pull requests, handoffs, or incidents.
Debug-Brief.problem: When developers fix a bug, the proof of what they tried, what failed, what finally passed, and which files changed lives only in scrollback and memory. Reconstructing that into a PR description, a handoff note, or an incident write-up is manual and lossy, and people routinely overstate results (claiming a test passed they never actually saw go green). DebugBrief captures the real debugging session as it happens and renders a report strictly from recorded evidence, so the write-up reflects what actually occurred. It explicitly does not use AI, infer a root cause, or report a test result you did not get (README lines 9, 65).
Debug-Brief.failureMode: The industry gap it addresses is fabricated or unverifiable claims in engineering write-ups: PR/handoff/incident notes that assert "tests pass" or imply causation without proof. DebugBrief's design counter is that pass/fail comes only from a real exit code, a command counts as verified only when a recognized runner actually exits 0, and file changes between a failing and passing check are reported as correlation, never proven cause (README lines 54, 63; derive.py _detect_red_to_green and _build_one_liner).
Debug-Brief.thesis: An honest debugging report should be derived only from recorded evidence, never inferred. DebugBrief is a dependency-light, Unix-only CLI that captures foreground commands under a pseudo-terminal and emits deterministic, evidence-only Markdown/JSON briefs with every empty section omitted and nothing invented.
Debug-Brief.howItWorks: A session is started (or auto-started by run/note). 'debugbrief run -- <cmd>' executes the command under two pseudo-terminals (one for stdout, one for stderr) in its own process group so output streams live while a bounded head-and-tail preview is retained; terminal escape sequences and unsafe control chars are stripped from the stored copy by a streaming state machine. The runner records the real exit code, duration, timestamps, and a per-command Git snapshot (HEAD + changed files), then classifies the command via token-pattern matching (filters.py recognizes pytest, jest, vitest, go test, cargo test, etc., and build/lint/typecheck tools like mypy/ruff/tsc), unwrapping wrappers (python -m, uv/poetry run, npx, pnpm/yarn exec). Pass/fail derives strictly from exit code; compound shell commands are never attributed to a single tool unless declared with --verify and the exit code is reliable (pipefail in effect, no failure-masking operator). Secrets matching known shapes are redacted before any write. On 'end', derive.py reconstructs the red-to-green window (same command, same cwd, fail then pass), reproduce/verify commands, a one-line summary, the observed error line, and ruled-out attempts, all from events; reporters render pr/handoff/incident Markdown (and optional JSON). State is written atomically under .debugbrief/ with 0700/0600 perms and symlink/special-file refusal.

executionFlow: start (or auto-start on run/note): create session, record initial Git state, locally ignore .debugbrief/ via .git/info/exclude
run -- <cmd>: acquire command lease, execute under pseudo-terminal in its own process group, stream live output
capture: real exit code, start/end timestamps, duration, bounded stdout/stderr previews, per-command Git snapshot (HEAD + changed files)
classify: token-pattern match test/build/lint runner (unwrap wrappers); compound shell handled via reliability rules; pass/fail strictly from exit code
redact: scrub secret-like values from command text and previews, then persist event atomically
redo: re-run last captured command in same cwd/shell mode; refuse if session file is Git-tracked (fail closed) or contains a redaction placeholder
end: capture final Git state; derive red-to-green window, reproduce/verify commands, one-liner, observed error, ruled-out attempts from events
render: emit pr/handoff/incident Markdown (and optional JSON), omitting every empty section; optionally print to stdout for piping to gh pr comment

executionFlow: Raw natural-language prompt arrives over the /ws/advanced WebSocket (api/main.py)
Normalize: prompt_normalizer fixes high-confidence typos, collapses whitespace, extracts structural signals (URLs, quoted content) — deterministic, no model call
Structured intent: intent_planner separates command vs content and builds BrowserIntent (target site/url, query, content, constraints, success condition, risk level, validation strategy)
Risk check: risk.py classifies the intent; high-impact actions (cart/checkout/send/submit/delete/post/commit/account changes) are gated behind a confirmation blocker unless options.confirmed
Plan one action: deterministic IntentPlanner routes high-confidence intents directly; otherwise ai_agent (Groq->Gemini->Ollama cascade) picks the single next action from intent + current page
Registry validation: action_registry validates the chosen action against known executable actions and required params (rejects non-executable planner concepts and unknown actions)
Execute: browser_engine.execute_action runs the action via Playwright, then observes the new PageState
Adaptive loop: task_orchestrator detects loops/idle patterns and attempts recovery, feeding last-failure feedback into the next step (max_steps cap)
Validate completion: validators gate the terminal state — 'completed' only with visible page evidence, else 'unverified', or a structured 'blocked' with a typed blocker and suggested next step
Persist + stream: results, steps, validation evidence and blockers are persisted to SQLite and streamed back to the frontend
Helm-browser-agent.failureMode: Browser agents commonly fake success — they type the raw instruction into a search box, mark a task 'done' just because a page loaded, or hallucinate actions the underlying engine cannot execute. They also collapse when a single LLM provider's free-tier quota runs out, and they will navigate to whatever URL an LLM emits (an injection / SSRF risk) since URL choices are influenced by untrusted page text.
Helm-browser-agent.howItWorks: Every task runs through one fixed pipeline rather than a raw prompt to the browser. A deterministic normalizer cleans the prompt; an intent planner produces a structured intent that separates command words from the content to act on and declares an explicit success condition. A risk layer pauses high-impact actions for confirmation. A deterministic planner handles high-confidence routes (direct URLs, site searches, common destinations); only when no high-confidence route applies does the LLM choose the single next action. Every action is validated against a central action registry (the engine can only run known actions), executed via Playwright, and the resulting page is observed. A loop detector triggers recovery. Completion is gated by evidence-based validators: a run is only 'completed' when a validator can prove the outcome from the visible page, otherwise it ends 'unverified' or 'blocked' with a structured, typed blocker. The LLM provider is a cascade (Groq -> Gemini -> local Ollama) with daily-quota short-circuiting so it keeps working when a free tier is exhausted. A deterministic SSRF guard checks every URL before navigation.

Helm-browser-agent.problem: Most natural-language browser agents are unreliable in ways that matter: they paste the raw instruction into a search box, declare success just because a page rendered, or emit actions the underlying engine cannot run. They also depend on a single LLM provider whose free-tier quota runs out, and they will navigate to whatever URL the model picks — including private or cloud-metadata addresses influenced by untrusted page text. Helm addresses this by forcing every task through a fixed, auditable pipeline (normalize, structured intent, risk gate, registry-validated action, observe, evidence-based validation) instead of a raw prompt-to-browser loop. The goal is honest outcomes: completed only with proof, otherwise unverified or a precise structured blocker.

contamination-checker.failureMode: Existing benchmark-contamination detectors tend to emit binary 'this was/was not in training' verdicts that overclaim, depend on cloud APIs or logits not always available, and rarely gate methods on what access a model actually exposes or pair Min-K-style scores with a matched control. ContamCheckr addresses this by producing a calibrated, caveated evidence score, gating each method on probed model capabilities, requiring a matched control for Min-K%++ to contribute, and running entirely locally/CPU-friendly.
contamination-checker.howItWorks: ContamCheckr loads a local HuggingFace causal language model and probes which signals it can produce (greedy generation, sequence log-likelihood, full next-token distribution). It then runs up to three independent evidence methods on a benchmark dataset: (1) Guided overlap — prompts the model to reproduce the second half of each example once with the dataset/split named and once generically, scoring both against the reference with ROUGE-L/exact/near match; the positive part of the guided-minus-general ROUGE-L gap is the index. (2) Min-K%++ — for each target token, normalizes the token log-prob against the probability-weighted mean and std of the full vocabulary distribution, averages the lowest-k% tokens, and only becomes a calibrated index by computing AUC between benchmark and a supplied matched control. (3) Canonical-order permutation test — shards examples in published order and compares canonical sequence likelihood against seeded random permutations to get a p-value. The three method indices are combined with fixed base weights (0.5 guided, 0.3 Min-K%++, 0.2 order), renormalized over whichever methods produced usable evidence, mapped to low/moderate/high evidence bands, and reported with a bootstrap confidence interval, caveats, and a list of skipped methods. Output is explicitly an evidence score, not a membership verdict.

limitations: Results depend on model capability, prompts, tokenization, sample size, and control quality (README Limitations).
A weak model can fail to reproduce memorized text; a capable model can produce overlap without memorization.
Min-K scores shift across domains/tokenizers/model families; Min-K%++ contributes nothing without a matched control.
Order tests can respond to ordinary topical/narrative coherence rather than memorization.
Paraphrasing, deduplication, fine-tuning, and deliberate evasion can all reduce detection.
Validation is a single seeded run on synthetic data with an unusually small model (tiny-gpt2); not evidence about real benchmarks or large models (docs/VALIDATION.md).
Guided overlap contributed zero in all three validation runs.
Output is an evidence signal, never proof of training-set membership.
No CI and not published to PyPI; install is from source only.
contamination-checker.oneLiner: A local, CPU-friendly auditor that produces a caveated evidence score for benchmark contamination in causal language models, combining guided reproduction, control-calibrated Min-K%++, and a canonical-order permutation test.
contamination-checker.problem: Large language models are increasingly trained on web-scraped corpora that may include the very benchmarks used to evaluate them, inflating reported scores. Existing approaches often overclaim binary membership verdicts and may require cloud access or signals a model does not expose. ContamCheckr instead audits a local HuggingFace causal LM and produces an evidence score with explicit caveats — guided reproduction overlap, control-calibrated Min-K%++, and a canonical-order permutation test — gating each method on the model's probed capabilities. It is explicitly framed as evidence with uncertainty, not proof that an example was in the training corpus.

reference.rhythmNotes: Density: high but disciplined. The site packs ~15 sections yet never feels cluttered because each section is gated by a full-width serif headline + thin border rule (border-b border-border / border-ink), so the eye gets a hard reset between blocks. Pacing alternates deliberately: dense record sections (ventures grid, publications, debate archive on newsprint) are interleaved with visual exhales (Instagram grid, video, moments gallery). Hierarchy is almost entirely typographic — a single high-contrast serif (font-black) at huge clamp() sizes (clamp(2.4rem,9.5vw,8rem) for the name, clamp(1.5rem,5vw,3.8rem) for section heads) does the heavy lifting; body and metadata recede in size and weight. Color is a restrained newspaper palette: cream/newsprint backgrounds, near-black ink (#1A1916), hairline borders — almost no chroma, which is what makes it read 'editorial' rather than 'startup'. Interaction quality: tasteful, not gimmicky — stat numbers count up on scroll (note the >0< placeholders that animate to 250+/284+/etc.), headings have opacity:0 + translateY/scale entrance transitions revealed on scroll. Motion is subtle, single-purpose, and reinforces the 'turning the page' metaphor rather than competing with content. Leading is tight (leading-none / leading-[0.88]) and tracking negative (-0.025em) on display type for a printed-headline feel. The masthead conceit (Vol./No./Est./live month-year) is carried consistently and is the single strongest taste signal.

reference.cautions: Do NOT copy any of the following from shivanshgupta.com. (1) Code/markup: their Tailwind class strings, the exact clamp() type-scale values, the ink color token #1A1916, the cream/newsprint/border token names, the opacity:0 + translateY/scale reveal keyframes, or any component structure — reimplement independently. (2) Copy: every headline is theirs ('Building at the frontier of AI, mathematics & philosophy', 'Four frontiers.', 'What I've built and led.', 'Things I've coded.', 'Published work.', 'Honors & awards.', 'Moments.', 'Essays & newsletter.', 'Let's work together.', 'Letters by Shivansh') — write entirely new copy in our own voice. (3) Assets: no portraits/photos (the hero must be an interactive systems plate, not a photo), no Instagram/moments imagery, no logos. (4) Data: never reuse their numbers (250+, 284+, 100+, 15+, 300+, 7+) or credentials (Team India, Eskom ISF, Young Changemaker 2025, the named papers/projects) — all metrics must be Hari's own and verifiable. (5) Identity semantics: their Publications are self-authored; OUR Research Desk must surface EXTERNAL papers, not present them as Hari's work — keep that distinction sharp to avoid implying false authorship. (6) Conceit risk: the masthead/Vol./No./Est. newspaper framing is a genre convention we can adopt, but match it with REAL editorial substance — a thin masthead over hype reads as costume. Borrow the rhythm (typographic hierarchy, hairline section rules, dense-vs-exhale pacing, subtle scroll-reveal + count-up motion, near-monochrome palette), not the artifacts. Keep motion subtle and single-purpose as they do; avoid turning the count-ups/reveals into gimmicks.

papers.title: WebArena: A Realistic Web Environment for Building Autonomous Agents
papers.abstractParaphrase: Introduces a self-hostable, reproducible web environment with functional websites (e-commerce, forums, content management, collaboration tools) plus realistic tasks and outcome-based functional-correctness checks for evaluating autonomous agents that operate a browser.
papers.whyItMatters: Provides a reproducible task suite and functional-correctness evaluation harness that a browser-agent evaluation system can adopt as a baseline environment and scoring methodology.

papers.title: WebShop: Towards Scalable Real-World Web Interaction with Grounded Language Agents
papers.abstractParaphrase: Presents a simulated e-commerce shopping environment with many products and crowdsourced instructions where agents must search, navigate, and act on web pages to fulfill purchase goals, evaluated against reward signals and human performance.
papers.whyItMatters: Demonstrates grounded web interaction with reward-based scoring, useful as a reference design for task definition and reward shaping in a browser-agent evaluation pipeline.

papers.title: Mind2Web: Towards a Generalist Agent for the Web
papers.abstractParaphrase: Offers a dataset of real-website tasks across many domains with recorded action sequences, intended to train and evaluate generalist agents that follow instructions to operate live web pages.
papers.whyItMatters: Supplies real-website trajectories and element-selection metrics that can inform how a browser-agent harness measures action prediction and step accuracy across diverse sites.

papers.title: AgentBench: Evaluating LLMs as Agents
papers.abstractParaphrase: A multi-environment benchmark spanning interactive settings such as operating systems, databases, web browsing, and games to measure how well language models act as reasoning and decision-making agents over multiple turns.
papers.whyItMatters: Establishes a multi-task framework and aggregate scoring conventions that a general agent-evaluation effort can reuse for cross-environment comparison.

papers.title: AgentBoard: An Analytical Evaluation Board of Multi-turn LLM Agents
papers.abstractParaphrase: Proposes an analytical evaluation framework with fine-grained progress metrics and an interactive board for diagnosing where multi-turn agents succeed or fail across partially observable tasks, beyond final success rates.
papers.whyItMatters: Introduces progress-rate and subgoal diagnostics that a general agent-evaluation system can adopt to surface partial-credit signals rather than only pass/fail outcomes.

papers.title: SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?
papers.abstractParaphrase: Constructs a benchmark from real GitHub issues and pull requests where a model must produce a patch that makes a repository's test suite pass, evaluating end-to-end software issue resolution against hidden tests.
papers.whyItMatters: Defines the patch-against-tests evaluation pattern central to a code-repair arena, including repository setup and test-based verification of generated fixes.

papers.title: Teaching Large Language Models to Self-Debug
papers.abstractParaphrase: Describes a prompting approach in which a model inspects its own generated code, explains it, and uses execution feedback to iteratively diagnose and correct errors without additional supervision.
papers.whyItMatters: Provides an execution-feedback self-correction loop that a code-repair arena can integrate as an iterative fix strategy and as a baseline for measuring repair effectiveness.

papers.title: Evaluating Large Language Models Trained on Code
papers.abstractParaphrase: Introduces the Codex code model and the HumanEval problem set, evaluating functional correctness of generated programs via unit tests and the pass@k metric rather than text similarity.
papers.whyItMatters: Originates the pass@k functional-correctness metric and unit-test execution methodology that underpin how generated or repaired code is scored in a code arena.

papers.title: LiveCodeBench: Holistic and Contamination Free Evaluation of Large Language Models for Code
papers.abstractParaphrase: Builds a continuously updated coding benchmark that collects problems released over time with known dates, enabling contamination-free evaluation by testing models only on problems published after their training cutoff, across tasks like generation, self-repair, and test prediction.
papers.whyItMatters: Demonstrates a release-date-windowing strategy for avoiding train-test overlap that a contamination checker can apply to keep code evaluations untainted.

papers.title: Rethinking Benchmark and Contamination for Language Models with Rephrased Samples
papers.abstractParaphrase: Shows that paraphrased or translated copies of benchmark items can evade standard n-gram overlap contamination checks while still inflating scores, and proposes a stronger LLM-based detector to catch such rephrased contamination.
papers.whyItMatters: Directly motivates detection logic for a contamination checker by exposing the limits of surface n-gram matching and offering a semantic-similarity detection alternative.

papers.title: Stop Uploading Test Data in Plain Text: Practical Strategies for Mitigating Data Contamination by Evaluation Benchmarks
papers.abstractParaphrase: Argues that public plain-text test sets leak into training corpora and recommends practical mitigations such as encryption, holding out data, and gating access to reduce future benchmark contamination.
papers.whyItMatters: Frames preventive practices (encryption, access control, canary strings) that a contamination checker and its data-handling policies can operationalize.

papers.title: Ragas: Automated Evaluation of Retrieval Augmented Generation
papers.abstractParaphrase: Proposes a reference-free framework that scores retrieval-augmented systems on dimensions like faithfulness, answer relevance, and context relevance using model-based judgments, allowing evaluation without hand-written ground truth.
papers.whyItMatters: Supplies reference-free retrieval and grounding metrics that a general evaluation system can adopt to assess context quality and answer faithfulness in RAG pipelines.

papers.title: ToolLLM: Facilitating Large Language Models to Master 16000+ Real-world APIs
papers.abstractParaphrase: Presents instruction data and a training plus evaluation pipeline covering thousands of real REST APIs, including a multi-step solution-search method and an automatic evaluator for judging whether tool-calling trajectories complete tasks.
papers.whyItMatters: Offers an automatic evaluator and large API-calling task set that a reliable-tool-use harness can reuse to measure correct tool selection and multi-step API execution.

papers.title: API-Bank: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Tool-Augmented LLMs
papers.abstractParaphrase: Introduces a benchmark with executable API calls and graded dialogue scenarios that test whether a model can decide when to call a tool, retrieve the right API, and invoke it correctly, along with metrics for each capability.
papers.whyItMatters: Provides per-step tool-use metrics (call decision, retrieval, correct invocation) that a reliable-tool-use evaluation can adopt to pinpoint where tool calls break down.
